# Questions in Livestream 83

Live Stream #83: Now is the Winter of Our Discontent

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:31</a> | Why does 1 Samuel mention both the temple and the king before Samuel is even born? | He said Hannah's words are prophetic, while `temple` in 1 Samuel 1 reflects the later writer describing the Shiloh cult complex with familiar language. | He reads Hannah's reference to the king and anointed one as prophetic rather than a simple anachronism, and notes that Samuel is being written from the monarchy looking back. For the `temple` language, he says Shiloh was more than a tent: it had cult buildings, altars, and a broader sanctuary complex, so a later writer could call it a temple while describing the pre-Jerusalem site. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=1038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:18</a> | Why are rabbits unclean in Leviticus 11:6, and should the passage say they eat grass rather than chew cud? | He said it should not be translated as eating grass. Rabbits do chew in a cud-like way through their digestive cycle, though not like cows. | He rejects the grass translation and explains the issue through rabbit digestion: rabbits produce ordinary droppings and cecal clusters, then eat the cecals to recover nutrients released from fermented hay. That behavior is not bovine rumination, but he says it is close enough to the ancient description of chewing cud to answer the common objection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=1406" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:26</a> | Why is Samson never credited with liberating Israel from the Philistines? | Because he did not. He struck the Philistines, but he was ultimately captured and failed to bring Israel lasting peace. | He says Samson's story ends with capture, humiliation, and revenge, not national liberation. The narrative is important because it leads into Samuel, Saul, and eventually David, but Samson himself is a failed judge who does not give Israel the period of peace credited to successful deliverers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=1581" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:21</a> | What happened to Mentuhotep II's mortuary temple? | He said very little remains because much of its stone was quarried away, especially for Hatshepsut's nearby complex. | He says the Deir el-Bahri site is still restricted and under excavation, and that very little of Mentuhotep II's temple survives. In his account, Hatshepsut's nearby temple reused or pillaged much of its stone, even though Mentuhotep's complex had been a distinctive mortuary temple and tomb with a unique layout, colonnaded areas, a pyramid-like superstructure, and a tomb beneath. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=1832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:32</a> | In Genesis 1:1, does the Hebrew support creation ex nihilo or building from chaos? | He did not take the chaos view. He said the language suggests a blank-canvas formlessness, while not by itself requiring creation ex nihilo. | He treats `tohu va-bohu` as emphasizing formlessness rather than chaos, describing the phrase almost as an intensified or specially crafted expression for a blank canvas. At the same time, he says Genesis 1:1 does not by itself require ex nihilo creation, because the verse could function as a title or resumptive beginning for God's ordering work on earth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=2156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:56</a> | Did Snefru use labor practices resembling American slavery, and how severe was slavery under him? | He said Snefru likely used war slaves from Canaan for pyramid projects, but this was not the same as American chattel slavery. | He argues Snefru probably took war captives from Canaan and used them on multiple pyramid projects, since Egyptian labor alone likely could not support that scale of work. He distinguishes this from American chattel slavery, saying ancient slaves had different social expectations and were usually maintained as part of a household, while Snefru's use of unskilled captive labor may help explain structural problems in projects such as the Bent and Meidum pyramids. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=2481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:21</a> | Were girls in ancient Israel married around age 12 or 13, and how should that be morally evaluated? | He said the ancient context was shaped by survival, mortality, family protection, and arranged marriages, so modern categories should not be imposed simplistically. | He frames early marriage as part of a wider ancient survival context, not only an Israelite practice: mortality, childbirth risk, household economics, and lack of a social safety net all shaped marriage decisions. He says parents saw arranging a good marriage as protection for children who might otherwise be left vulnerable, so modern moral categories should not be projected onto the ancient world without accounting for those pressures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=3095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:35</a> | How should one answer a Hebrew Israelite claim that egyptology is false if the first Egyptians were not black, and that pyramids were not BCE because the Bible does not say so? | He described Egyptians as broadly Hamito-Semitic with regional variation and said Egyptian pyramid building clearly belongs before the Common Era. | He says ancient Egyptians varied by region within a Hamito-Semitic spectrum, with Delta and Ramesside examples leaning more Semitic and Nubian or Twenty-fifth Dynasty rulers showing more African traits. On pyramids, he dates Egyptian pyramid building from Djoser around the third millennium BCE through Ahmose I in the early Eighteenth Dynasty, so the Egyptian pyramid age is clearly before the Common Era. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=3510" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:30</a> | Are there extra-biblical historical records of Moab, Ammon, and Edom being judged like Babylon or Tyre? | He said not really. Moab, Ammon, and Edom have limited surviving texts, so the biblical record remains the primary source for much of this material. | He says the surrounding nations have left only limited written material: the Mesha Stele for Moab, small texts, seals, and seal impressions, but not large historical archives. Edom has somewhat more later material through Idumean history, yet for Ammon and Moab especially the biblical record remains the main historical source for judgment traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=3703" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:43</a> | What scholars, books, or historians would he recommend for studying the historicity of Judges? | He recommended Kenneth Kitchen's work on Old Testament reliability and Richard Hess's `Ancient Israelite Religion`, then following their bibliographies. | He points first to Kenneth Kitchen, especially for detailed argumentation about Judges and Old Testament reliability, while admitting the book is aging but has not really been surpassed. He also recommends Richard Hess's `Ancient Israelite Religion` for pre-monarchy Israelite religion, then suggests using both books' bibliographies for deeper study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=3856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:16</a> | If full preterists appeal to Scripture alone against the creeds, should Christians listen to the church, and is sola scriptura at fault? | He said full preterism is outside orthodox faith, but sola scriptura is not nuda scriptura: church tradition is a consulting voice, while Scripture has final authority. | He says full preterism historically arose from counter-Reformation Roman Catholic interpretation rather than from Protestant sola scriptura, and that its denial of the future second coming and final judgment puts it outside orthodox faith. His distinction is that sola scriptura still consults creeds, councils, and church fathers, but does not treat them as inspired or as able to overrule Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=4518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:18</a> | What advice does he have for overcoming vices such as lust, gluttony, and pride? | He distinguished ancient gluttony, righteous and unrighteous pride, and lust inside versus outside marriage, then emphasized Scripture, prayer, and spiritual discipline. | He first narrows the categories: ancient gluttony meant consuming another person's allotment in a scarcity society, pride can be righteous self-knowledge or sinful hubris, and sexual desire is good within marriage but distorted outside it. His practical counsel is to read and meditate on Scripture, pray because prayer changes and conforms the believer's mind, and rely on spiritual disciplines through which the Holy Spirit works against the flesh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=5231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:11</a> | How can Jesus be said to have been born in 4 BC? | He said it is a calendar artifact: Dionysius Exiguus miscalculated the epoch, and later evidence placed Jesus's birth a few years earlier. | He explains that the BC/AD system was created without a year zero and with Jesus's birth treated as the epoch boundary. Later chronological work, including evidence around Herod's death and an eclipse mentioned by Josephus, showed that Dionysius Exiguus had placed the epoch a few years late, so the calendar label remains even though Jesus's actual birth was earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=5429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:29</a> | Did ancient Egyptians think they came from somewhere other than Egypt? | He said no. Egyptian myth presented Egyptians as native to the land, created and placed in Egypt rather than migrating from elsewhere. | He says Egyptian self-understanding did not include an origin story like an exodus or migration from Mesopotamia or Africa into Egypt. Instead, Egyptian myth pictured Egyptians as people of the land, connected to Kemet and primordial divine activity, with the gods making humanity and placing them in Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=5586" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:06</a> | When is he going to play guitar on stream? | He said he uses the guitar mostly as a composition tool, not for live performance, so viewers should not expect him to play on stream. | He says he is more of a composer than a live player, and chose his SG-style guitar for feel and usefulness with a digital audio workstation rather than for performance. His music is recorded and manipulated for composition tests, so the instrument is a tool rather than something he plans to shred on stream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=5821" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:01</a> | Does Genesis 19:36 mean there were no Moabites or Ammonites before Lot's daughters gave birth to their ancestors? | He said not necessarily. The sons of Lot are presented as pivotal defining figures for those nations, not necessarily their only biological origin. | He says Moab and Ammon are treated as Abrahamic relatives of Israel, which explains why some neighboring peoples were off limits for Israelite invasion. The sons born to Lot's daughters need not be the only biological origin of those peoples; rather, they became pivotal figures whose connection to Lot defined the nations' character and relationship to Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=6057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:57</a> | Were the Exodus plagues polemics against Egyptian gods? | He said no. He traced that view to a modern scholarly theory and argued that it lacks both strong Egyptological support and direct biblical warrant. | He traces the plague-as-polemic view to a modern theory that assigned each plague to Egyptian deities, but says the details break down and the Bible only directly links the gods of Egypt to the final plague in Exodus 12:12. He suggests that `gods` there may include Egypt's human power holders, such as priests, officials, judges, and Pharaoh, whose firstborn heirs represented the power base being judged. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=6605" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:05</a> | Is there a secret ancient library under the Sphinx? | He said no. Ground checks and restoration work show no such passages or library under the Sphinx, though he wished more texts existed. | He says people have searched around the Sphinx with digging, even destructive nineteenth-century attempts, and modern ground-penetrating radar and restoration work have not revealed passages beneath it. He allows that undiscovered passages near associated temples are more conceivable, but says the Sphinx itself is bedrock, despite his personal wish for a lost library of texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=6860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:20</a> | What does he think of Dorothy Eady's reincarnation claims, and would possession by ancient dead spirits conflict with Scripture? | He rejected reincarnation as unbiblical, said consulting the dead is forbidden, and suggested possession is possible but not by deceased human spirits. | He rejects reincarnation because Scripture says humans die once and then face judgment, and he treats attempted communication with the dead as forbidden rather than harmless. He distinguishes ghostly phenomena from possession, saying possession would involve other spirits rather than deceased humans and would be an unholy act contrary to Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=7377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:57</a> | Does a claim about Haman as an Egyptian priest in the Quran prove the Quran true? | He said no. He treated the claim as unsupported and said he knew of no such Egyptian record from the reign of Ramses II. | He says this is why original sources matter, because he knows of no Egyptian record for the claimed priest from the reign of Ramses II. He treats the argument as an Islamic story rather than evidence and rejects it as proving the Quran's account. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=7614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:54</a> | How can the clan explanation work with biblical frameworks such as Adam being 130 when Seth was born or Noah's sons entering the ark at 100? | He said Hebrew literature can aggregate figures or retroject famous progenitors, so several clan-based explanations could account for those details. | He explains that Hebrew literature can treat multiple people or a clan line as one representative figure, as with an idealized or aggregated Pharaoh in Exodus. For Adam, Eve, Seth, and Noah's sons, he suggests possibilities such as multiple women being aggregated under `Eve` or famous progenitors being retrojected backward after their clan identity becomes dominant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=7979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:59</a> | Has he seen the Indiana Jones trailer? | He had seen it and thought it looked bad, especially because Harrison Ford no longer fit the role as an action hero. | He says he had finally watched the trailer and reacted very negatively, mainly because Harrison Ford no longer looked plausible to him as the whip-cracking action version of Indiana Jones. He jokes that an emeritus-professor version might work, but the action framing made him less interested in seeing the film. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=8117" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:17</a> | How did Moses know about Adam, Noah, and Abraham? | He said Moses likely knew through oral tradition passed down among the sons of Jacob, with oral transmission preserving narratives while updating vocabulary. | He says Moses likely received these narratives through oral storytelling among the sons of Jacob rather than through a written record we can identify. He notes that oral tradition can preserve narrative accurately over time while still updating vocabulary or details in retelling, so it can be stable and flexible at the same time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=8248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:28</a> | If the Star of Bethlehem appeared after the Messiah's birth, how did Herod determine the child's age range? | He said the Magi were seeking a recently born king, so Herod used their report and travel timing to target infants two years old and younger. | He says the Magi's search was for one who had been born king of the Jews, and their travel time helped Herod estimate a broad infant age range. Herod did not need exact astronomical dating; his paranoia and the Magi's report led him to target children two years old and under in Bethlehem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=8423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:23</a> | What are his thoughts on replacement theology? | He said replacement theology has serious problems because Romans 11 shows God is not done with Israel, and not all promises to Israel transfer to the church. | He defines replacement theology as the church taking Israel's place and inheriting all Israel's promises, then says Romans 11 argues against God being finished with Israel. Christians share in the relationship promise through Abraham, but he says land and posterity promises remain tied to the sons of Jacob and that Messianic Jewish growth also weighs against a simple replacement model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=8685" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:45</a> | How does he handle Joshua 11:11 in light of his statement about Hazor not being burned in Judges 4? | He said Joshua 11 and Judges 4 refer to different periods. His point was that Judges 4 does not claim Hazor was burned in Deborah's time. | He clarifies that he was not denying Joshua 11:11, which does describe Hazor being burned under Joshua. His argument was that Judges 4, centuries later in Deborah's time, refers to Hazor's armies but does not claim the city was burned, conquered, or destroyed then. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=9062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:02</a> | Does Genesis 1:28's use of `subdue` carry violent connotations? | He said he would need a fuller word study, but the term seems to involve force or coercion, with violence possible by extension. | He is cautious because he has not done a full word study, but he says the term appears to involve force, coercion, and making the wild world submit for human rule and food production. On that basis violence may be possible by extension, though he does not state it as a settled lexical conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=9191" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:11</a> | Do aquariums and bird cages have a long history, and how far back do they go? | He said fish ponds are ancient, true glass aquariums are much later, and bird cages go back to ancient Egypt. | He distinguishes fish ponds, which are ancient, from glass aquariums, which require sheet glass and appear much later, around the early modern period. Bird cages, by contrast, go back to ancient Egypt, where people kept birds, including pigeons and birds of prey, and examples of cages are known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=9401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:41</a> | What is a one-liner response to the claim that there is no archaeological evidence for the Exodus? | He said to ask what evidence exists instead, because absence of evidence is not itself evidence against the Exodus and positive evidence still needs explanation. | He says the claim is an assertion, not an argument, and the response should press for what evidence would count and what positive evidence exists instead. He argues that absence of evidence is not evidence against something unless there is positive evidence for a different scenario, and points to items such as Avaris, Merneptah, highland settlements, Mount Ebal, and Egyptian-linked traditions as data needing explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=9754" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:34</a> | What might a new Reformation in the church look like? | He said the exact form is hard to predict, but it would need a renewed esteem for Scripture and responsible, regular Bible reading. | He says reformations and revivals often take unexpected forms, so the exact institutional shape is speculative. The constant he expects is a return to Scripture: reading it regularly, esteeming it, learning how to read it responsibly in a twenty-first-century setting, and letting that renewed attention reshape affections toward God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=10112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:32</a> | What does he think of reading Genesis 1:1 as a restricted relative clause? | He was not comfortable with it and said it feels like an unnatural way for a Semitic text of this kind to begin. | He says he respects the scholar making the argument but is not currently persuaded, because the reading feels grammatically and literarily unnatural for a Semitic opening. He prefers understanding the phrase as a prepositional introduction or title, while leaving room to study the technical grammar more deeply before making a final judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=10318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:58</a> | How does the missing tomb of Cleopatra compare with the tombs of other Ptolemaic rulers? | He said most Ptolemaic tombs are also unaccounted for, so finding those first would be a better strategy for narrowing Cleopatra's burial location. | He says almost all Ptolemaic royal tombs are missing, with only one possible candidate and even that controversial. If he were searching for Cleopatra, he would first look for tombs of the later Ptolemies, because locating that burial context would make Cleopatra's likely burial area easier to narrow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=10633" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:13</a> | How did the Egyptian language evolve, and how is Demotic both a script and a late form of Egyptian? | He distinguished language stages from scripts, saying Demotic is mainly a script for Ptolemaic Egyptian and is often loosely conflated with the language. | He lays out language stages as Old Egyptian, Middle Egyptian, Late Egyptian, Ptolemaic Egyptian, and Coptic, then separates those from scripts such as hieroglyphic, hieratic, Demotic, and Coptic script. Demotic is primarily a ligature-based script often used for Ptolemaic Egyptian, especially administrative documents, so people loosely conflate script and language even though they are distinct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=11108" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:08</a> | Does God approve Jehu's destruction of Ahab's house in 2 Kings 10:30 but condemn it in Hosea 1:4? | He said it is not a contradiction: Jehu did some commanded judgment, but also went beyond the commission in the bloodshed at Jezreel. | He reads 2 Kings 10 as approving Jehu's obedience in executing judgment on Ahab's house and granting his line four generations on Israel's throne. Hosea's condemnation, in his reading, addresses Jehu taking matters too far in Jezreel's bloodshed, especially the wider slaughter of Ahab's sons beyond the immediate commission. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=11800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:40</a> | Will he make a video responding to comparisons between Jesus and Osiris? | He said maybe, but popcorn-review targets are chosen selectively based on length, topic, educational value, and copyright risk. | He does not commit to a specific target, saying the channel is selective about review videos. Suitable candidates are usually educational, on YouTube, around 30 to 45 minutes, interesting enough to be useful, and unlikely to create copyright or legal problems, so suggestions may be considered without guaranteeing a response. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=11949" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:09</a> | Is he aware of the Galilean earthquake of 363 AD, and does he think God intervened to stop construction of the Third Temple? | He said he was aware of the earthquake, but thought it more clearly affected the Temple of Jupiter and did not prove a stopped Third Temple project. | He says the earthquake did damage the Temple of Jupiter and that he can see providence in that, but he doubts it proves a Third Temple construction project was stopped. He thinks Rome likely would not have allowed such a temple anyway, and that God can work through natural means without the event serving the specific claim in the question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=12160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:40</a> | What are his thoughts on claims that the Old Testament is developing henotheism, historical allegory, or too human in quality? | He rejected the Old Testament as mere historical allegory or developing henotheism, favored monarchical monotheism, and said human qualities are expected in Scripture addressed to humans. | He says the Old Testament contains symbolism and typology but is also religious history, not merely allegory, and he distrusts allegorical methods when they start with theology rather than the text. He favors monarchical monotheism over a simple development from henotheism, and says human qualities in Scripture are appropriate because God communicates through human language to human readers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=12751" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:31</a> | Could the Egyptians really do magic like the Westcar Papyrus and Exodus suggest? | He said Egyptians practiced many things called magic, from sleight of hand to ritual and pharmacology, but it is hard to know what was actually effective. | He says Egyptian texts and practices show many forms of magic: sleight of hand, execration rituals, curses, amulets, potions, and possibly applied pharmacology. Because those categories coexist and observers are not available, he says it is difficult to know which effects were tricks, ritual, medicine, demonic activity, or something else. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=13116" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:36</a> | What was Jesus doing before his public ministry? | He said Jesus worked as a carpenter, likely in the normal inherited trade of his father's household. | He says Jesus was known as the son of a carpenter, which in that world meant learning the father's trade. He explains that carpentry could include furniture or building components such as roof beams, and that the trade may also have shaped where Joseph and Mary could find work when fleeing to Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=13426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:46</a> | Who is more knowledgeable, Indiana Jones or Titus Kennedy? | He declined to answer directly because of the recent controversy, saying viewers could come to their own conclusion. | He refuses to answer directly because people were still upset about the recent controversy and he did not want to aggravate it further. He says viewers can draw their own conclusion and jokes that he is pleading the fifth on the Indiana Jones versus Titus Kennedy question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=13533" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:33</a> | Why does secularism feel tired and limiting? | He said the problem is a shallow, fundamentalist-style way of viewing the world, which can also appear in some forms of Christianity. | He says the tiredness comes from a one-way, restricted worldview that cannot take in other ideas or withstand scrutiny well. He applies that critique especially to evangelical atheism or fundamentalist-style secularism, while noting that some Christians also become trite and limited in the same way and that some secular thinkers are more open and deep. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=13756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:16</a> | What are his thoughts on Richard Friedman's Levite Exodus theory? | He was skeptical, mainly because he rejects the documentary hypothesis framework and sees timing and distribution problems with a small Levite-only Exodus. | He says Friedman's Exodus argument depends heavily on the documentary hypothesis, which he rejects, so much of the Levite-only theory becomes unnecessary to him. He also sees a practical timing problem: it is hard to explain the widespread late thirteenth- and early twelfth-century Israelite settlements by a small Levite group distributing itself through Canaan and the Judah foothills. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=14001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:21</a> | What does 1 Peter 3:7 mean by calling women the weaker vessel? | He said it is a euphemism for a precious, fine vessel such as glassware: something honored and cared for, not treated roughly. | He interprets the phrase as an image of fine Roman glass or delicate ceramics: precious, beautiful, and meant for honored use rather than rough treatment. The point is not contempt for weakness but a command that husbands grant honor and care, treating wives as fellow heirs rather than abusing or disregarding them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=14427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:27</a> | What does he think of Messianic Judaism holding to Old Testament traditions and practices, and how does that relate to Galatians? | He said Messianic Judaism varies widely. Some groups are healthy, but legalistic versions resemble the kind of law-dependence Paul opposed in Galatians. | He says Messianic Judaism ranges from healthy evangelical-like congregations that honor Jewish traditions to groups nearly indistinguishable from legalistic Hebrew Roots movements. Practices such as feasts, Sabbath, Hanukkah, or dietary observance may be fine if not required for salvation, but Galatians becomes relevant when law-keeping, circumcision, kosher rules, or separation from Gentiles are treated as necessary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/46uDNoLqNA8?t=14875" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:55</a> | How should one answer claims that Ramses II spoke with Haman, the high priest of Amun, to build a tower and then drowned? | He said there is no evidence for that Quranic version of the Exodus and no biblical warrant for Pharaoh drowning. | He says there is no proof that such a Haman existed and suggests some claims confuse titles with names, just as `Pharaoh` is a title rather than a personal name. His larger objection is that the Islamic version has Pharaoh drowning, which he says has neither evidence nor biblical warrant. |
